elements.sent-2411-001: Recent observations of exotic hadrons have been stimulating the theoretical investigation of the internal structure of hadrons.
elements.sent-2411-002: While all hadrons are eventually composed of quarks and gluons by the strong interaction, clustering phenomena of quarks can generate hadronic molecules---weakly bound systems of hadrons---which are expected to emerge near two-hadron thresholds.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, it should be noted that a pure hadronic molecule is not realized, as the strong interaction induces mixing with other possible configurations.
elements.sent-2411-004: The compositeness of hadrons has been developed as a promising concept to quantitatively characterize the fraction of the hadronic molecular component.
elements.sent-2411-005: Here we summarize the modern understanding of the compositeness to study the internal structure of hadrons, and review the applications of the compositeness to various quantum systems in different energy scales, such as nuclei and atomic systems in addition to hadrons.
elements.sent-2411-006: In recent hadron spectroscopy, thanks to the rapid experimental developments, the number of observed hadrons keep increasing~\cite{ParticleDataGroup:2024cfk .}
elements.sent-2411-007: In particular, it is remarkable that many candidates of the exotic hadrons are found, starting from the observation of the charmonium like state $X(3872)$~\cite{Belle:2003nnu to the recent findings of the pentaquarks $P_{c}$~\cite{LHCb:2015yax,LHCb:2019kea and the tetraquark $T_{cc}$~\cite{LHCb:2021vvq,LHCb:2021auc .}}}
elements.sent-2411-008: These newly observed hadrons are not well fit in the traditional classification of hadrons, namely, $\bar{q q$ mesons and $qqq$ baryons~\cite{Hosaka:2016pey,Guo:2017jvc,Brambilla:2019esw,Chen:2022asf .}}
elements.sent-2411-009: This triggers the intensive discussion on the internal structure of hadrons such as multiquark states, hadronic molecules, and gluon hybrids.
elements.sent-2411-010: Among others, hadronic molecules are of particular interest from the viewpoints of the clustering phenomena.
elements.sent-2411-011: In contrast to the ordinary hadrons which are made from quarks and gluons by the QCD interactions, the main building blocks of the hadronic molecules are the ground state hadrons (constituent hadrons), and the hadron-hadron interaction is the driving force to make self-bound systems.
elements.sent-2411-012: Because these constituent hadrons are composed of quarks, the hadronic molecules can be regarded as the clustering of quarks.
elements.sent-2411-013: As a universal feature of the clustering~\cite{PTPS52.89 , it is expected that the hadronic molecules are formed if the binding energy of the two hadrons is much smaller than the binding of quarks in the constituent hadrons.}
elements.sent-2411-014: The weak binding nature allows for the constituent hadrons to keep their identity as hadrons.
elements.sent-2411-015: But the clustering of quarks has two peculiar features which are not shared by the clustering of other quantum systems in different hierarchies, such as nuclei and atomic systems:.
elements.sent-2411-016: Color confinement: quarks are confined in hadrons, so the ``binding energy'' of quarks in a hadron is not well defined.
elements.sent-2411-017: Also, the clustering into the non-color-singlet subunit is (at least asymptotically) not possible.
elements.sent-2411-018: Pair creation and annihilation: quark-antiquark pairs can be created and annihilated by the strong interaction.
elements.sent-2411-019: This leads to the mixing of the states with different number of particles, such as $\bar{q q\leftrightarrow \bar{q}\bar{q qq$.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: These features make the discussion of hadronic molecules interesting but also challenging.
elements.sent-2411-021: It should be emphasized that the hadronic molecules cannot be distinguished from the normal hadrons by the conserved quantum numbers.
elements.sent-2411-022: For the discussion of the hadronic molecules, therefore, we need to introduce some measure to quantify the internal structure.
elements.sent-2411-023: It is the compositeness that draws attention recently in this context.
elements.sent-2411-024: The idea of the compositeness has appeared in the discussion to distinguish between the composite and elementary particles in the 1960s~\cite{Weinberg:1962hj,Weinberg:1963zza,Weinberg:1964zza,Weinberg:1965zz .}
elements.sent-2411-025: In the recent revival of the compositeness for exotic hadrons, it is important to incorporate the unstable nature of the hadrons, because the most of the candidates of hadronic molecules are unstable against the strong decay.
elements.sent-2411-026: In this paper, we overview the theoretical foundation of the study of the compositeness of hadrons, including the recent developments.
elements.sent-2411-027: A brief survey as of 2013 can be found in a review article~\cite{Hyodo:2013nka , written by one of the present authors (see also Refs.~\cite{Guo:2017jvc,Oller:2017alp,vanKolck:2022lqz from other viewpoints).}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Since then, the notion of the compositeness has been widely applied in hadron physics.
elements.sent-2411-029: Moreover, thanks to the low-energy universality, the compositeness can also be used for the systems in different hierarchies.
elements.sent-2411-030: This paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-031: In section~\ref{sec:history , we summarize the history of the study of the compositeness of hadrons.}
elements.sent-2411-032: The basic formulation of the compositeness is introduced in section~\ref{sec:formulation in the framework of nonrelativistic quantum mechanics.}
elements.sent-2411-033: We emphasizes the model dependence of the compositeness in various expressions, and present the generalization to higher partial waves and to the coupled-channel scattering.
elements.sent-2411-034: Section~\ref{sec:WBR deals with the weak-binding limit where the structure of a weakly bound state in $s$ wave is model-independently determined.}
elements.sent-2411-035: We demonstrate how the model dependence of the compositeness is suppressed in this limit.
elements.sent-2411-036: The results in sections~\ref{sec:formulation and \ref{sec:WBR are revisited from the viewpoint of the effective field theory in section~\ref{sec:EFT .}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: We demonstrate that some issues in sections~\ref{sec:formulation and \ref{sec:WBR can be well understood in the formulation with the field theory.}}
elements.sent-2411-038: The generalization to unstable resonances is discussed in Section~\ref{sec:unstable .}
elements.sent-2411-039: Several interpretation schemes of the complex compositeness are summarized.
elements.sent-2411-040: Applications to various physical systems, including hadrons, nuclei, and atomic systems, are presented in section~\ref{sec:applications .}
elements.sent-2411-041: One of the central problems in particle physics in the 1960s is to distinguish the composite particles from the elementary ones.
elements.sent-2411-042: A series of works by Weinberg~\cite{Weinberg:1962hj,Weinberg:1963zza,Weinberg:1964zza,Weinberg:1965zz appeared in this context, and it was shown that the vanishing of the field renormalization constant ($Z=0$) can be used as a criterion to characterize the composite particle, and the renormalization constant can be related to the scattering length and the effective range for a weakly bound state (weak-binding relation), with the successful application to the deuteron.}

elements.sent-2411-712: For a shallow bound state, the compositeness can be determined in a model-independent manner, thanks to the low-energy universality.
elements.sent-2411-713: We survey the attempts to generalize of the compositeness to unstable resonances, which is needed for the application to exotic hadrons.
elements.sent-2411-714: As applications, the compositeness is found to be useful for studying the composite nature of the exotic hadrons, including light scalar mesons, $\Lambda(1405)$, $X(3872)$, and $T_{cc}$.
